column7: ARGUMENTUM (ARGUMENT TO THE BOOKS OF KINGS). The Books of Kings follow in right temporal order immediately after the Book of Judges, whose appendix is the Book of Ruth. For after the Judges there reign Kings in Israel, whose deeds are described here — hence the books are called the Books of Kings. The Book of Judges ends with Samson, who before Eli was the last Judge, ruling twenty years (Judg. 16 end). Immediately succeeding Samson was Eli the High Priest, who ruled Israel as a quasi-Judge for forty years. Eli was succeeded by Samuel and Saul for the same number of years. Samuel was therefore absolutely the last Judge, for under him the people asked for a King, whom Samuel by God's command first gave them in Saul. He thus changed the state of the Republic from the aristocracy of the Judges to the monarchy of the Kings. Eli's deeds are narrated not in Judges but in this Book of Kings, because under him flourished Samuel the Prophet, who transformed the principate into a kingdom. The Holy Spirit in the four Books of Kings pursues the history of the Jewish people and of the Church or old Synagogue, immediately from the exit of the Judges. The first book treats of the birth, upbringing, and principate of Samuel, and of the reign, deeds, and death of Saul; the second treats of the reign of David, who was substituted by God's command for Saul (rejected by God for disobedience). These two first books are called by the Hebrews the Books of Samuel — either because they begin with Samuel's deeds, or because Saul's and David's deeds are reckoned Samuel's deeds, since Samuel constituted them as kings. They cover 110 years: Eli ruled 40 years; Samuel and David likewise 40 years each. The two later books (Third and Fourth Kings) narrate the deeds of Solomon and his sons and grandsons who reigned in Judah from Solomon to the last Zedekiah; also the deeds of Jeroboam and his successors who made schism with Rehoboam and Judah and reigned in Israel until the last King Hosea, taken captive to Assyria by Shalmaneser in the sixth year of Hezekiah. So these two books span 430 years. S. Gregory (Proem.): "The history of the Kings, drawn up by the Holy Spirit in the most sacred volumes of Holy Scripture, should not be thought inferior in mysteries to any work of lesser authority. For all its writers are reported to have been prophets, who while narrating historical matters signify spiritual things; speaking outwardly, they animate within; proposing earthly things, they draw men toward heavenly things." And Augustine (De Civit. XVII.1): "Scripture, which by the order of kings narrates their deeds and events, seems engaged in historical diligence with reporting past events; but when pondered with God's Spirit aiding, it is found intent on foreannouncing future things rather than commemorating past."

FUIT VIR UNUS DE RAMATHAIM SOPHIM (There was a certain man of Ramathaim-sophim). "Vir unus" — singular, distinguished, extraordinary, as if one among many. RAMATHAIM-SOPHIM: This city, the fatherland of Elcana and Samuel, was called Rama (meaning "height," because situated on a mountain), and Ramatha, and Ramathaim (dual form = "two heights"), because the city was divided and situated on a twin-peaked hill. So Serarius; though Sanchez thinks Ramathaim is so called in the dual because the city was near another Rama (which was in the tribe of Benjamin), as if the two cities, by reason of their proximity, coalesced into one body. The same city, with the added letter A, is called Arimathia, from which came Joseph, the noble decurion who buried Christ (Matt. 27) — as S. Jerome, Bede, Angelomus, Rabanus, Hugo, Dionysius, and others assert. It is also called Sophim (="watchmen"), either because there were watchtowers and garrison-sentinels on the high mountain, or because (per S. Jerome, Lyranus, and R. Solomon) it was a town of "speculators," i.e., contemplatives — prophets and learned men devoted to the study of Sacred Scripture. Hence the Chaldaean renders: "A man of Ramatha, one of the disciples of the prophets." For Elias, Eliseus, and their disciples dwelt on Carmel and other mountains, to flee the crowds, to be free for God alone in solitude, and to look up more readily from the heights toward heaven.

EXULTAVIT COR MEUM IN DOMINO (My heart rejoiced in the Lord). This is Anna's canticle, or as S. Augustine calls it, a hymn, in which she gives thanks for past and present benefits and prophesies of future things in Christ (vv. 5 and 10). It is therefore partly a Eucharistic canticle and partly a prophecy, as Theodoret and Procopius say; though S. Augustine (De Civitate XVII.4) wishes the whole to be a prophecy of Christ and the Church — which is true in the Allegorical sense. For Anna, says Theodoret and Procopius, because she conceived a prophet, also participated in the grace of prophecy, as did Elizabeth and the Blessed Virgin. Hence the Canticle of Zechariah at the birth of John the Baptist, and the canticle of the Blessed Virgin at the conception of Christ — "Magnificat anima mea Dominum" — are similar to this canticle of Anna and harmonize with it word for word, as one may see by comparing them. Hear what S. Bernard writes of the canticle (Serm. 1 in Cant.): "There is no sound of those bursting with desire, no sound of lamenting, but of rejoicing; not of one despairing, but trusting; not of words heard in public, but of the bride and groom singing together and hearing each other." From what has been said it is established that this canticle was inspired in Anna by the Holy Spirit and is a part of Sacred Scripture.
